[discussion] Discussion PC originating from CRC was once considered a stage IV condition with limited survival prospects, often associated with a very poor prognosis compared to metastases at other sites. In recent years, CRS with or without HIPEC has emerged as a promising approach for achieving long-term survival among patients with peritoneal metastatic disease. Nonetheless, the outcomes of recent randomized trials, such as Prodige-7, have failed to demonstrate survival benefits from CRS with or without HIPEC [ 5 ], necessitating a careful selection of the patient population that will benefit from these treatments [ 24 ]. Identifying prognostic factors is important for refining patient selection. For example, primary tumor location is now considered a stratification factor for survival [ 25 ]. In addition, PC arising from appendiceal cancer is known to differ from CRC in terms of distinct clinical and biological behavior [ 26 ]. However, there is a controversy in the literature regarding the impact of tumor location on prognosis. Both PCI and CC scores have been shown to influence survival following CRS and HIPEC in multiple studies [ 27 , 28 ]. Among them, PCI is by far the most important prognostic factor [ 28 ]. Researchers often use the PCI score to decide whether CRS and HIPEC are appropriate treatment options. However, these scores require intraoperative exploration or preoperative imaging examinations, and sometimes, the scores obtained through preoperative imaging examinations may not be consistent with intraoperative scores, thus negating the potential benefits of PCI score as a preoperative basis for selecting CRS + HIPEC treatment. Serological markers possess the advantages of convenience and cost-effectiveness. However, many uncertainties persist regarding their use in predicting the prognosis of patients with PC of CRC. In this study, we evaluated the prognostic impact of serological markers based on our center’s experience. Furthermore, we compared the impact of serological markers with PCI scores on the prognosis of patients with PC of colorectal cancer. The relationship between inflammation and tumors has always been a captivating research area, and previous studies have shown that systemic inflammatory response plays a crucial role in the development and metastasis of tumors [ 29 ]. Owing to the crucial role of neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, and platelets in tumor induced systemic inflammatory response, these markers have the potential to reflect the broader systemic inflammatory response in patients with cancer [ 30 ]. NLR, PLR, and MLR are the most commonly used predictors of all systemic inflammatory indicators. These indicators have been reported in many tumors, including lung cancer, cervical cancer, breast cancer, and CRC [ 31 – 34 ]. Previous studies have identified correlations between NLR, PLR, and factors such as tumor size, staging, and lymph node metastasis, establishing their utility for early diagnosis and prognosis evaluation of patients with CRC [ 35 ]. Additionally, MLR levels in postoperative peripheral blood have emerged as independent prognostic factors in conditions such as colon cancer [ 36 ] and other tumor types. These indicators reflect the inflammatory response during the cancer process and the interaction between cancer cells and immune system components [ 29 ]. However, there has been little research on the role of these inflammatory factors in patients with peritoneal metastasis of CRC. For many years, PC was synonymous with an incurable disease. However, advances in CRS and HIPEC changed the prognosis of PC, with the median survival extending from 13 to 63 months [ 37 ]. Our study contributes to this improvement by showing a median OS of 52.63 months and a median PFS of 27.03 months. Importantly, both the PCI score and CC score continue to be prognostic indicators for patients with peritoneal metastasis of CRC, which is consistent with previous studies. In addition to these traditional discoveries, our study found that some inflammatory markers and tumor markers are also associated with the prognosis of patients with peritoneal metastatic cancer. Notably, Kaplan–Meier survival analysis underscored the significance of NLR, PCI, and CA 19–9 for both OS and PFS. Meanwhile, MLR and CEA only showed an association with OS. However, PLR did not show any significant association with survival. The results of univariate Cox regression analysis were consistent with these findings. In the multivariate analysis, NLR, PCI, and CA 19–9 emerged as significant prognostic markers in terms of both OS and PFS. Similar observations have been reported by Ihemelandu et al., where PLR and CA 19–9 were identified as significant prognostic indicators of survival [ 38 ]. Rangarajan et al. found that patients with high NLR have poorer survival and proposed NLR as a reliable tool for predicting the outcomes of CRS and HIPEC for pseudomxyoma peritonei of appendiceal origin [ 36 ]. Another study indicated that preoperative PLR and CEA are associated with OS in patients with PC or CRC undergoing CRS and HIPEC [ 39 ]. Zager et al. reported that patients with higher NLR and lower LMR have worse overall survival after CRS + HIPEC, highlighting the potential of LMR in selecting patients who can benefit from this treatment [ 20 ]. A recent study focusing on patients undergoing CRS for CRC peritoneal metastases suggests that a higher NLR is associated with poorer OS, whereas a higher PLR predicts favorable DFS [ 40 ]. The exact mechanism by which these inflammatory factors exert their predictive effect on the prognosis of certain tumors is still unclear. Currently, it is widely recognized that inflammation plays a crucial role in the formation and development of tumors [ 41 ]. Nonetheless, there is still disagreement on which inflammatory indicators can provide the most effective prediction for clinical practice. Further exploration is needed to determine how these biomarkers should be synergistically employed for prognostication and to identify their optimal cutoff values [ 29 ]. In addition, this study included traditional predictive factors, revealing that, in multivariate analysis, PCI, CA 19–9, and CC0 status were independent prognostic factors. However, the prognostic role of CEA appeared relatively weak. There have been some reports on the predictive value of CEA and CA 19–9 in early-stage peritoneal metastatic cancer. For instance, Aziz et al. found that CEA < 6 is associated with significantly higher OS and DFS in patients with peritoneal metastasis originating from the appendix undergoing CRS and HIPEC, whereas CA 19–9 < 38 is not associated with OS or DFS [ 42 ]. Another study reported that CA 19–9 > 500 is associated with a poorer OS in patients with peritoneal metastasis originating from colonic sites [ 38 ]. In our study, patients with CA 19–9 > 36.65 had significantly poorer OS and PFS. The results of ROC curve analysis showed that the PCI continues to be the most robust prognostic indicator. Meanwhile, CA 19–9 is also a valuable prognostic factor for both OS and PFS, with NLR showing certain advantages in predicting PFS. There are different reports on the predictive value of CEA and CA 19–9, along with different cut-off values of these biomarkers, which accentuates the need for further clarity. In addition, whether patients with appendiceal origin should be studied as a separate subgroup warrants further exploration. This study has several limitations that should be acknowledged. Firstly, this was a retrospective single-center study with a small sample size, which may have resulted in insufficient statistical power. Secondly, some patients underwent chemotherapy or targeted drug therapy in other centers after undergoing CRS + HIPEC, and we do not have complete data in this regard. Thirdly, owing to the small sample size, this study did not separate patients based on whether the peritoneal metastasis originated from the appendix or non-appendix sources for analysis. Tumors of appendiceal origin and non-appendiceal origin exhibit differences in prognosis. However, due to the limited sample size in this study, we were unable to perform separate analyses for these two groups, which may introduce potential bias to the conclusions. We are continuing to collect cases of peritoneal metastatic carcinomas and plan to conduct subgroup analyses based on tumor origin in future studies. Fourth, although all procedures were performed by the same specialized surgical team to minimize variability, differences in individual patient conditions (e.g., disease burden) may still influence outcomes. In summary, our research findings underscore the independent prognostic significance of NLR, PCI, and CA 19–9 in patients undergoing CRS + HIPEC for peritoneal metastasis of CRC. However, these findings should be further validated through large multicenter studies to strengthen their clinical implications.

[conclusion] Conclusions In conclusion, identifying patients with PC originating from CRC who will benefit from CRS and HIPEC, as well as those who may require additional treatments, is essential for the management of this disease. Our study found that NLR and CA 19–9 could potentially be valuable prognostic markers in patients with peritoneal metastasis of CRC undergoing CRS and HIPEC, though their utility needs to be further verified in larger prospective cohort studies. The potential integration of these two markers with other traditional indicators might enhance the screening of patients who could optimally benefit from CRS and HIPEC treatment. These findings provide a theoretical basis for more individualized treatment and patient screening, offering a promising avenue for improved clinical decision-making.

retrieved_papers.title: The role of multi-modality adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation in women with advanced stage endometrial cancer.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2007]
[Abstract] : The optimal adjuvant therapy for women with stages III and IV endometrial cancer following surgical staging and cytoreductive surgery is controversial. We sought to determine the outcome of patients with advanced stage endometrial cancer treated with postoperative chemotherapy+/-radiation to determine whether there was an advantage to combining treatment modalities.
: A retrospective analysis of patients with surgical stages III and IV endometrial cancer from 1975 to 2006 was conducted at Duke University and the University of North Carolina. Inclusion criteria were comprehensive staging procedure including hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, +/-selective pelvic/aortic lymphadenectomy, surgical debulking, and treatment with adjuvant chemotherapy and/or radiotherapy. Progression-free (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were analyzed using Kaplan-Meier method and Cox proportional hazards model.
: 356 Patients with advanced stage endometrial cancer were identified who received postoperative adjuvant therapies; 48% (n=171) radiotherapy alone, 29% (n=102) chemotherapy alone, 23% (n=83) chemotherapy and radiation. The median age was 66 years; 38% had endometrioid tumors; and 83% were optimally debulked. There was a significant difference between the adjuvant treatment groups for both OS and PFS (p<0.001), with those receiving chemotherapy alone having poorer 3-year OS (33%) and PFS (19%) compared to either radiotherapy alone (70% and 59%) or combination therapy (79% and 62%). After adjusting for stage, age, grade, and debulking status the hazard ratio (HR) for OS was 1.60 (95% CI, 0.88 to 2.89; p=0.122) for chemotherapy alone and 2.01 (95% CI, 1.17 to 3.48; p=0.012) for radiotherapy alone, compared to combination therapy. When the analysis was restricted to optimally debulked patients the adjusted HR for patients who were treated with either chemotherapy or radiation alone indicated a significantly higher risk for disease progression [HR=1.84 (95% CI, 1.03 to 3.27; p=0.038); HR=1.80 (95% CI, 1.10 to 2.95; p=0.020)] and death [HR=2.33 (95% CI, 1.12 to 4.86; p=0.024); HR=2.64 (95% CI, 1.38 to 5.07; p=0.004)], respectively, compared to patients who received combination therapy.
: Combined adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation was associated with improved survival in patients with advanced stage disease compared to either modality alone. Future clinical trials are needed to prospectively evaluate multi-modality adjuvant therapy in women with advanced staged endometrial cancer to determine the appropriate sequencing and types of chemotherapy and radiation.

[Introduction] 1 Introduction Endometrial cancer stands as the most prevalent gynecologic malignancy in the United States, with a projected 67,880 new cases diagnosed in 2024 alone ( Uterine Corpus, 2024 ). While many patients present with early-stage disease and experience favorable outcomes, a significant proportion face a high-risk diagnosis, characterized by increased recurrence and mortality rates. This heterogeneous group includes patients with advanced stage, high grade tumors, and/or aggressive histologic subtypes such as serous or clear cell carcinomas. Additionally, certain molecular features can confer a high-risk designation, although incorporating these classifications into clinical practice has yet to become standard of care. This high-risk subset of patients faces a considerably elevated risk of disease recurrence and mortality, presenting a challenge to healthcare providers and a need for effective adjuvant treatment methods. Historically, adjuvant treatment for high-risk endometrial cancer was pelvic or whole abdominal radiotherapy. While this modality was successful at decreasing pelvic recurrences of disease, it failed to significantly impact the rate of distant recurrence or overall survival ( Creutzberg et al., 2000 , Keys et al., 2004 ). To determine if chemotherapy could offer a survival advantage over radiation in high-risk endometrial cancer, the randomized GOG-122 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in both progression-free and overall survival with chemotherapy compared to whole abdominal radiation. However, recurrence rates were 54 % and 51 % in the chemotherapy and radiation arms, respectively, and the local recurrence rate in the chemotherapy arm was as high as 18 % ( Randall et al., 2006 ), prompting exploration of combined chemoradiation to prevent both local and distance recurrence and improve overall survival. Subsequent studies such as GOG 184 ( Homesley et al., 2009 ) supported this approach, showing decreases in both distant and local recurrence rates with the combination of radiation therapy and chemotherapy, and 3-year relapse-free survival rates of greater than 60 %. Other studies have also reported superior outcomes with the combination of chemotherapy and radiation therapy in these high-risk patients ( Lupe et al., 2009 , Gehrig et al., 2004 , Hoskins et al., 2001 , Alvarez Secord et al., 2007 , Onda et al., 1997 ). While systemic chemotherapy is the foundation of treatment for high-risk endometrial cancer patients, these data would suggest that combination therapy is reasonable. National guidelines such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) recommend systemic chemotherapy with consideration of radiation therapy since optimal adjuvant therapy in these patients remains elusive. Two studies that examined the role of chemoradiation in high-risk endometrial cancer patients include PORTEC-3 and GOG-258. PORTEC-3 was initiated to investigate the benefit of adjuvant chemotherapy during and after radiation compared to radiation treatment alone in this patient population. This study found that, while the addition of chemotherapy to radiation did not improve overall survival in their overall study cohort and led to an increased risk of grade 3 adverse events, specific groups including stage III and serous cohorts showed improved failure-free survival with the addition of chemotherapy ( de Boer et al., 2018 , de Boer et al., 2019 ). GOG-258 evaluated the benefit of chemoradiation compared to chemotherapy alone and found that chemoradiation did not portend improved survival compared to chemotherapy alone ( Matei et al., 2019 ). While both trials have contributed valuable insights, their differing study designs have left clinicians without a clear answer regarding the optimal adjuvant approach. Without a direct comparison of all three modalities (radiation, chemotherapy, and combination chemoradiation), uncertainty persists about which strategy offers the best chance of long-term survival for patients with high-risk endometrial cancer. Moreover, the strict protocols of clinical trials often diverge from real-world clinical practice, where treatment variations are common. To address these gaps in knowledge, we conducted a comprehensive analysis using the National Cancer Database (NCDB), a real-world dataset capturing a broad spectrum of patient demographics and treatment patterns. We sought to directly compare the survival benefit of different adjuvant treatment modalities for high-risk endometrial cancer, including adjuvant radiation alone, adjuvant chemotherapy alone, adjuvant concurrent chemoradiation, and adjuvant sequential chemotherapy with radiation therapy (sequential chemoradiation). To accomplish this, we leveraged the available data in the NCDB, which captures comprehensive information on cancer diagnoses, treatments, and outcomes from a broad range of healthcare settings. Its real-world data provides a valuable complement to the findings of randomized controlled trials and offers insights into the effectiveness of these treatment modalities in diverse patient populations and clinical practices. By identifying the adjuvant treatment strategy associated with the best survival benefit, we can equip clinicians with the evidence-based information needed to make more informed treatment decisions. We hypothesized that patients receiving combined modality treatment (either concurrent or sequential chemoradiation) would have improved overall survival compared to those receiving chemotherapy or radiation alone.

[introduction] 1 Introduction Endometrial cancer stands as the most prevalent gynecologic malignancy in the United States, with a projected 67,880 new cases diagnosed in 2024 alone ( Uterine Corpus, 2024 ). While many patients present with early-stage disease and experience favorable outcomes, a significant proportion face a high-risk diagnosis, characterized by increased recurrence and mortality rates. This heterogeneous group includes patients with advanced stage, high grade tumors, and/or aggressive histologic subtypes such as serous or clear cell carcinomas. Additionally, certain molecular features can confer a high-risk designation, although incorporating these classifications into clinical practice has yet to become standard of care. This high-risk subset of patients faces a considerably elevated risk of disease recurrence and mortality, presenting a challenge to healthcare providers and a need for effective adjuvant treatment methods. Historically, adjuvant treatment for high-risk endometrial cancer was pelvic or whole abdominal radiotherapy. While this modality was successful at decreasing pelvic recurrences of disease, it failed to significantly impact the rate of distant recurrence or overall survival ( Creutzberg et al., 2000 , Keys et al., 2004 ). To determine if chemotherapy could offer a survival advantage over radiation in high-risk endometrial cancer, the randomized GOG-122 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in both progression-free and overall survival with chemotherapy compared to whole abdominal radiation. However, recurrence rates were 54 % and 51 % in the chemotherapy and radiation arms, respectively, and the local recurrence rate in the chemotherapy arm was as high as 18 % ( Randall et al., 2006 ), prompting exploration of combined chemoradiation to prevent both local and distance recurrence and improve overall survival. Subsequent studies such as GOG 184 ( Homesley et al., 2009 ) supported this approach, showing decreases in both distant and local recurrence rates with the combination of radiation therapy and chemotherapy, and 3-year relapse-free survival rates of greater than 60 %. Other studies have also reported superior outcomes with the combination of chemotherapy and radiation therapy in these high-risk patients ( Lupe et al., 2009 , Gehrig et al., 2004 , Hoskins et al., 2001 , Alvarez Secord et al., 2007 , Onda et al., 1997 ). While systemic chemotherapy is the foundation of treatment for high-risk endometrial cancer patients, these data would suggest that combination therapy is reasonable. National guidelines such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) recommend systemic chemotherapy with consideration of radiation therapy since optimal adjuvant therapy in these patients remains elusive. Two studies that examined the role of chemoradiation in high-risk endometrial cancer patients include PORTEC-3 and GOG-258. PORTEC-3 was initiated to investigate the benefit of adjuvant chemotherapy during and after radiation compared to radiation treatment alone in this patient population. This study found that, while the addition of chemotherapy to radiation did not improve overall survival in their overall study cohort and led to an increased risk of grade 3 adverse events, specific groups including stage III and serous cohorts showed improved failure-free survival with the addition of chemotherapy ( de Boer et al., 2018 , de Boer et al., 2019 ). GOG-258 evaluated the benefit of chemoradiation compared to chemotherapy alone and found that chemoradiation did not portend improved survival compared to chemotherapy alone ( Matei et al., 2019 ). While both trials have contributed valuable insights, their differing study designs have left clinicians without a clear answer regarding the optimal adjuvant approach. Without a direct comparison of all three modalities (radiation, chemotherapy, and combination chemoradiation), uncertainty persists about which strategy offers the best chance of long-term survival for patients with high-risk endometrial cancer. Moreover, the strict protocols of clinical trials often diverge from real-world clinical practice, where treatment variations are common. To address these gaps in knowledge, we conducted a comprehensive analysis using the National Cancer Database (NCDB), a real-world dataset capturing a broad spectrum of patient demographics and treatment patterns. We sought to directly compare the survival benefit of different adjuvant treatment modalities for high-risk endometrial cancer, including adjuvant radiation alone, adjuvant chemotherapy alone, adjuvant concurrent chemoradiation, and adjuvant sequential chemotherapy with radiation therapy (sequential chemoradiation). To accomplish this, we leveraged the available data in the NCDB, which captures comprehensive information on cancer diagnoses, treatments, and outcomes from a broad range of healthcare settings. Its real-world data provides a valuable complement to the findings of randomized controlled trials and offers insights into the effectiveness of these treatment modalities in diverse patient populations and clinical practices. By identifying the adjuvant treatment strategy associated with the best survival benefit, we can equip clinicians with the evidence-based information needed to make more informed treatment decisions. We hypothesized that patients receiving combined modality treatment (either concurrent or sequential chemoradiation) would have improved overall survival compared to those receiving chemotherapy or radiation alone.

[methods] 2 Methods 2.1 Data source The study utilized data from the NCDB Participant User Files, a comprehensive cancer registry that captures information on approximately 70 % of newly diagnosed cancer cases in the United States. All data are de-identified, and thus the study was considered exempt from the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center Institutional Review Board. 2.2 Study population This retrospective cohort study included patients diagnosed with stage II-IVa endometrioid endometrial cancer and stage I-IVa serous or clear cell endometrial cancer between 2004 and 2020 who underwent primary treatment with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingoophorectomy. Patients with unknown or missing data were excluded. Although PORTEC3 included patients with stage IB grade 3 and/or LVSI endometrioid endometrial carcinoma, we excluded patients with stage I endometrioid histology due to limitations in the NCDB that prevent reliable identification of deep myometrial invasion and LVSI status. In an attempt to standardize the type of adjuvant treatment received in our patient population, we determined strict inclusion criteria for each adjuvant treatment modality. For adjuvant chemotherapy alone, we included only patients that received chemotherapy within 70 days (10 weeks) from surgery. For adjuvant radiation, we included only patients that received radiation within 56 days (8 weeks) from surgery. For concurrent chemoradiation, we included only patients who received their first chemotherapy and radiation treatments within 7 days of one another and first dose of radiation within 56 days (8 weeks) from surgery. For sequential chemoradiation, we included patients who either received radiation therapy followed by chemotherapy or chemotherapy followed by radiation therapy. For sequential radiation followed by chemotherapy, we included patients who had received their first dose of radiation within 56 days (8 weeks) from surgery, and chemotherapy within 35–56 days from radiation (5–8 weeks). For sequential chemotherapy followed by radiation, we included patients that received their first dose of chemotherapy within 70 days (10 weeks) from surgery, and radiation within 70–126 days (10–18 weeks) from chemotherapy (see Supplementary Material ). For patients that received adjuvant radiation , EBRT and VBT are presented as distinct groups (see Supplementary Material ), however, some patients may have received both. Our primary analysis categorized patients by the primary adjuvant radiation modality. 2.3 Study variables The primary exposure variable was adjuvant treatment modality, which included adjuvant radiation alone, adjuvant chemotherapy alone, adjuvant sequential chemoradiation, adjuvant concurrent chemoradiation, and no adjuvant treatment. The primary outcome variable was overall survival, defined as the time from the date of diagnosis to the date of death or the last follow-up date. Other variables included patient demographics, facility characteristics, and tumor characteristics. 2.4 Statistical analysis Baseline patient demographics, facility characteristics, and tumor characteristics among patients in each adjuvant treatment modality group were reported and compared among adjuvant treatment modality groups. For continuous variables, means and Standard Deviation (SD) were presented, and group comparisons were conducted using analysis of variance (ANOVA). For categorical variables, frequency and percentage were reported and compared using Chi-square test. To identify the factors associated with OS, we constructed univariable Cox proportional hazards models, each including one independent variable: adjuvant treatment modality, age, race, type of insurance, stage, grade, histology, facility type, facility location, and residential setting. P-values, crude hazard ratios (HR) and the 95 % confidence intervals (CI) are reported. To compare the effects of different adjuvant treatment modality groups on OS, potential confounding effects were adjusted for using the propensity score weighting method. Propensity scores were estimated using a multivariable multinomial regression model with the following covariates: age, race, insurance, stage, facility type, facility location, residential setting, and histology. The marginal HR and 95 % CI of treatment were obtained from the Cox proportional hazards model weighted by propensity score. This procedure was completed with R package Weightit ( Greifer, 2024 ) . A two-sided P-value of < 0.05 was considered statistically significant in all tests. All statistical analyses were performed using R version 4.3.0.

[results] 3 Results A total of 48,831 patients met inclusion criteria for the study (see Supplementary Material ). Table 1 shows baseline patient demographics, facility characteristics, and tumor characteristics among patients in each adjuvant treatment modality group. Patients were divided into three age groups according to GOG-99 high-intermediate risk criteria for endometrial cancer: <50 years old (N = 3554, 7.3 %), 50–69 years old (N = 27,778, 56.9 %), and 70 + years old (N = 17,499, 35.8 %). The majority of patients had endometrioid histology (N = 27,739, 56.8 %), followed by serous (N = 17,368, 35.6 %), followed by clear cell (N = 3,724, 7.6 %). A total of 19,251 patients received no adjuvant treatment, 8,076 received adjuvant radiation only, 13,418 received adjuvant chemotherapy only, 1,849 received adjuvant concurrent chemoradiation, and 6,237 received adjuvant sequential chemotherapy and radiation. Within the sequential chemotherapy and radiation group, 5,991 patient received chemotherapy first followed by radiation, and 246 received radiation first followed by chemotherapy. There was a significant difference in adjuvant treatment modality received among age, race, insurance status groups, stage, grade, facility type, facility locations, and residential setting. Of note, among patients with stage IV disease, 36 % did not receive adjuvant therapy. This may reflect selection bias related to unmeasured clinical factors such as poor performance status or treatment-limiting comorbidities. The absence of adjuvant treatment in this subgroup could also be attributable to palliative intent. These missing data may introduce bias, potentially inflating the observed benefit of adjuvant modalities in other groups. Table 1 Baseline characteristics, grouped by treatment. Of the 16,162 patients who received adjuvant radiation, either alone or with chemotherapy, the majority (68.0 %, n = 10,985) underwent external beam radiotherapy (EBRT), while 31.3 % (n = 5,051) received vaginal cuff brachytherapy (VCB). The type of radiation was unknown for a small proportion of patients (0.4 %, n = 72). The median radiation dose was 4500 cGy (IQR: 4500–4600) (see Supplementary Material ). The dose of radiation was unknown for N = 3,313 patients. On univariable analysis, younger age, Hispanic and Asian race, private insurance, stage II disease, grade 1 disease, facility location in the Western US, and metropolitan residential setting, were all associated with improved OS ( Table 2 ). From the univariable-Cox proportional hazards regression, adjuvant radiation (HR 0.61, 95 % CI 0.59–0.64, p < 0.001), concurrent chemoradiation (HR 0.65, 95 % CI 0.60–0.72, p < 0.001), and sequential chemoradiation (HR 0.63, 95 % CI 0.60–0.66, p < 0.001) were associated with improved survival compared to no adjuvant treatment and compared to adjuvant chemotherapy alone (HR 0.89, 95 % CI 0.86–0.93, p < 0.001, Table 2 ). Chemotherapy alone was also associated with improved survival compared to no adjuvant treatment (HR 0.85, 95 % CI 0.81–0.89, p < 0.001, Fig. 1 ), however, when compared to concurrent and sequential chemoradiation, had a worse survival (HR 1.22, 95 % CI 1.06–1.41, p = 0.006 and HR 1.35, 95 % CI 1.25–1.45, p < 0.001, respectively). Chemotherapy alone was associated with a modest but statistically significant decrease in overall survival compared to radiation alone (HR 1.10, 95 % CI 1.01–1.21, p = 0.027). Fig. 1 Box plot showing marginal hazard ratios of adjuvant treatment modalities from the propenisty score −weighted Cox proportional hazards model. Subgroup analyses of propensity-weighted data revealed distinct patterns by histology and stage. For endometrioid tumors, adjuvant radiation alone was associated with the best survival outcomes in stage II disease (HR 0.80, 95 % CI 0.76–0.85, p < 0.001), while adjuvant concurrent and sequential chemoradiation were superior in stage III disease (HR 0.55, 95 % CI 0.53–0.57, p < 0.001; HR 0.49, 95 % CI 0.47–0.51, p < 0.001, Fig. 2 ). Chemotherapy alone was associated with worse survival in endometrioid stage II disease (HR 1.55, 95 % CI 1.26–1.90, p < 0.001). Of the 4,313 stage III endometrioid patients who underwent adjuvant concurrent or sequential chemoradiation, 3,766 (87.3 %) received EBRT while 547 (12.7 %) received VCB (see Supplementary Material ). The stage IV endometrioid group (N = 326) were predominantly treated with chemotherapy alone (N = 160, 49 %) or no adjuvant treatment (N = 117, 35.9 %), limiting robust analysis of other adjuvant treatment modalities ( Fig. 2 ). For serous tumors, sequential chemoradiation was associated with the best outcomes in both stage II and III disease, (HR 0.57, 95 % CI 0.44–0.75, p < 0.001; HR 0.53, 95 % CI 0.46–0.60, p < 0.001, Fig. 3 ). In stage III serous tumors, adjuvant chemotherapy alone showed significantly worse survival compared to sequential chemoradiation (HR 1.27, 95 % CI 1.2–1.34, p < 0.001). However, neither concurrent nor sequential chemoradiation significantly differed from chemotherapy alone in the stage II cohort (p = 0.927 and 0.162, respectively). Of the 1,290 patients with stage II and III serous tumors who underwent adjuvant concurrent or sequential chemoradiation, 1,017 (78.8 %) received EBRT while 273 (21.2 %) received VCB. While concurrent chemoradiation demonstrated the best survival in stage I serous tumors (HR 0.49, 0.30–0.49, p = 0.004), there were only 111 patients in this subgroup that were treated with concurrent chemoradiation, and this finding did not significantly differ from outcomes of sequential chemoradiation (p = 0.13) or chemotherapy alone (p = 0.164, Fig. 3 ). The stage IV serous group (N = 205) were similarly treated predominantly with adjuvant chemotherapy alone (N = 149, 72.7 %), or no adjuvant treatment (N = 50, 24.3 %), which again limited analysis of other treatment modalities ( Fig. 3 ). In the clear cell cohort, adjuvant sequential chemoradiation showed the best survival (HR 0.70, 95 % CI 0.56–0.89, p = 0.003, see Supplementary Material ), although this did not differ significantly from adjuvant chemotherapy alone (p = 0.288). Of the 407 clear cell patients that received adjuvant sequential chemoradiation, 234 (57.5 %) received EBRT and 173 (42.5 %) received VCB (see Supplementary Material ).
